title: Spray-applied and liquid-applied airtightness membranes
subtitle: Where sprayed airtightness coatings are used and how they work
excerpt: Liquid-applied airtightness membranes are sprayed or rolled onto masonry, concrete, or timber substrates to form a continuous air barrier across irregular surfaces. They are used where sheet membranes are difficult to detail at penetrations and junctions, and in new-build or major renovation projects targeting very low airtightness results.
sourceNotes: BBA and ETA product certification for Pro Clima Kaflex, Siga Majcoat, and similar spray-applied systems. ATTMA TS1:2016 on airtightness test requirements. Passive House Institute guidance on airtightness layers in EnerPHit retrofit projects.

glossaryTerms.definition: A continuous layer in the building envelope that resists the passage of air. Can be formed by plaster, membranes, foil-faced boards, or a spray-applied coating.
A Passive House Institute certification standard for the deep retrofit of existing buildings to a very high level of energy performance, including a blower-door test result of 1.0 m³/h/m² or below.

content.text: Sheet airtightness membranes work well where the substrate is flat and regular. On a brick wall with joist pockets, service entries, existing socket boxes, and irregular mortar joints, a sheet membrane needs dozens of individual tape seals at penetrations. A spray-applied
conforms to the substrate and seals irregular surfaces in one pass, then receives the insulation system over the top.

Acrylic-based spray membranes: applied cold with an airless sprayer or brush/roller for small areas. Cure to a flexible film that bridges hairline cracks. Application thickness 1 to 2 mm wet (0.5 to 1 mm dry). Not vapour-open; suited to masonry substrates that will be covered with insulation.

Vapour-open spray membranes: applied similarly but use a formulation with controlled vapour permeance (typically Sd value 0.5 to 1.0 m). Suited to timber-frame or pre-1919 masonry where vapour management through the membrane is needed.

Closed-cell spray polyurethane foam: applied as a thicker layer (25 to 50 mm) to provide both air barrier and thermal insulation in one operation. Very high airtightness performance but difficult to reverse; the substrate is coated permanently.
retrofits that need below 0.6 air changes per hour at 50 Pascals. Tape-and-sheet cannot achieve this over rough masonry.
Junctions where new timber frame meets existing masonry: mixed substrates make sheet membrane detailing difficult without a movement gap.
Historic masonry interiors where the surface is too irregular for sheet membranes.

Spray membranes require a clean, dust-free, dry substrate. Surface contamination is the most common cause of delamination failures. The manufacturer's minimum and maximum application temperature must be observed; most water-based products cannot be applied below 5 degrees Celsius or above 30 degrees Celsius. A peel-adhesion test (a test patch applied, cured, and peeled after 24 hours) on the actual substrate is good practice before a large application. The cured film should resist peeling without tearing; if the film peels cleanly from the substrate, the surface needs a primer or de-dusting.
